title: Çoban salatası
subtitle: Turkish shepherd's salad with tomato, cucumber, and sumac
excerpt: Tomato, cucumber, green pepper, red onion, and parsley dressed with lemon juice and olive oil — Turkey's simplest and most widely eaten salad. No cooking, ten minutes from start to finish. The vegetables should be very finely diced so each forkful contains all four.
sourceNotes: Çoban salatası (shepherd's salad) is documented in Turkish household cooking literature from the early twentieth century and is considered one of the staple preparations of the Turkish table, present at nearly every meal. Similar chopped vegetable salads appear throughout the broader Levantine and Eastern Mediterranean culinary tradition. The name refers to the simple, pastoral origin of the dish: tomatoes, cucumbers, and onions from the garden, eaten by shepherds in the fields.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made just before serving; the vegetables release water if dressed more than 20 minutes ahead.

content.text: Çoban salatası lives or dies by the knife work. The vegetables should be diced small and uniformly (about 1 cm or smaller) so that each forkful contains all four components in proportion. A coarsely chopped version where you eat a mouthful of tomato followed by a mouthful of cucumber is a different dish. Take the time to dice properly.

Dress the salad just before serving. The vegetables release water quickly once salted and dressed, and the salad deteriorates within 20 minutes. If you need to prepare in advance, dice the vegetables and keep them dry; dress at the table. The dressing is simply lemon juice, salt, and olive oil: nothing more.

Dice the {{tomato}} tomatoes and {{cucumber}} cucumber to 1 cm pieces. Finely dice the {{red-onion}} onion and the {{pepper-green}} green pepper. Combine in a large bowl. Add the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Squeeze the {{lemon}} juice over, drizzle with the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, and add a generous pinch of salt. Toss gently. Scatter the {{sumac}} over if using. Taste for salt and lemon, then serve immediately.

items.symptom: Salad is watery and the dressing has disappeared
items.cause: Dressed too far in advance; vegetables release water quickly once salted
items.fix: Dress within 10 minutes of serving; if preparing ahead, keep the vegetables dry and dress at the table

items.symptom: Onion taste is too sharp and overpowering
items.cause: Red onion not rinsed; raw onion is very pungent
items.fix: Rinse the diced red onion under cold water for 30 seconds and pat dry before adding; this removes the harshest sulphur compounds

items.symptom: Salad lacks flavour despite being dressed
items.cause: Undersalted; or out-of-season tomatoes with no flavour
items.fix: Salt generously — the dressing should coat everything; use ripe, in-season tomatoes when possible; a squeeze of extra lemon often helps

items.symptom: Pieces are uneven and some vegetables dominate
items.fix: Take the time to dice all vegetables to the same 1 cm size; the salad should taste like all ingredients at once

content.text: Çoban salatası appears at nearly every Turkish meal as a matter of course: alongside grilled meats, with soups and pides, next to rice and lentil dishes, as part of meze spreads. It is the default salad of the Turkish table in the way that a green salad is the default in France or a tomato-based salad in Italy. The same combination of tomato, cucumber, onion, and parsley with a lemon-oil dressing appears throughout the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean under different names (Israeli salad, Arab salad, Shirazi salad in Iran), evidence of a shared agricultural and culinary tradition that predates national boundaries.
